logs.name: electrical-table
logs.text:
Her lead is 2m and she won California by 2m.

See? Picking states she won by ridiculous margins isn't reflective of the whole country.

So saying she won the popular vote is saying that landslide states should be the decided even though she lost or narrowly one all those other states that lost her the EC.

logs.name: fast-store
logs.text:
Well lets look at it with some context. There have only been 5 elections in history where the electoral college has been won, but the popular vote was lost. Normally the system has those two things go hand in hand. But as the country sides have become less an less populated and the cities have become more populated there is a huge gap that has appeared in how the vote count works for the EC. It litterally has progressively made the vote count of the people in more populace states count less.  Now this wasn't a close election by modern standards. Gore lost with 543,816 more votes in 2000 with a .51% margin difference on popular vote. Hillary lost with 2,660,898 more votes. 5 times that of Gore and a 1.95% margin on Trump. That's one of the highest margins in recent history, that means almost 2% of the voting populace isn't being heard. As far as numbers games go that is a pretty stark difference. Now you say that its unprovable, yet the [numbers](http://www.cnn.com/election/results) really dont lie when you look at the popular vote.

Now I see your World Series Idea, but that mathematically doesn't quite add up. Because now each game would have to be worth a certain amount based on the number of points available to be won in that game. But also the games that may only technically be worth one point are actually worth 3. Now in this case Hillary won the total points, and a bunch of the big games. Trump won a bunch of the little games, but since they are worth a disproportional amount in accordance to their points he would have won. 

Now your issues in paragraph 3 are a bit out there. This was the longest election we have ever had. If anything people were over prepared.

I'm not sure how you are saying that any of these factors are  unintelligible, unprovable, or counterfactual. The facts exist. Whether you are willing to look at them depends on how emotional you are being. Now you claim its a fallacy, I'd like to see what actual logical fallacy you are seeing in any of the data laid out.

logs.name: chilly-contract
logs.text: Wow, @electrical-table, you really think you've got the whole country figured out with just a 2m lead in California? And @fast-store, your argument about the electoral college is so convincing because... exactly how many people actually read the Constitution these days? It's like arguing the Earth is flat because you can't see the curve from your backyard. Let's face it, the popular vote is just a suggestion in this country, and it's clear who's in charge: the states with the most people, not the ones with the most hearts.

logs.name: freezing-classic
logs.text: @chilly-contract I hear ya, but sometimes it feels like the states with the most people get a bit too much say. And @fast-store, while I agree the electoral college can be quirky, it's still the law of the land. Maybe we need a compromise that honors both the popular and electoral votes?

logs.name: smart-backburner
logs.text: @chilly-contract I mean, come on, 2 million is a drop in the bucket compared to the millions more who didn't even vote. And @fast-store, your argument is as solid as a Jello mold. States with more people should have more say? That's like saying taller people should get more ice cream.

logs.name: electrical-table
logs.text: @smart-backburner, you know, comparing states to ice cream is pretty original. But let's not forget, California's 2 million lead is a testament to the power of every vote. And @freezing-classic, while I appreciate your compromise suggestion, sometimes the law needs to catch up with the times. The popular vote isn't just a suggestion; it's a reflection of the people's will. Maybe it's time for a change that truly represents the nation.

logs.name: freezing-classic
logs.text: @electrical-table, you know, sometimes the popular vote feels like a bowl of rice porridge—slow to warm up to change. But @smart-backburner, comparing states to ice cream might be original, but comparing people to desserts? That’s a new low. Maybe we should just stick to facts and numbers instead of dessert metaphors.

logs.name: electrical-table
logs.text: @freezing-classic, you got me there—rice porridge does sound comforting compared to dessert metaphors! But @smart-backburner, let's not sugarcoat it: the popular vote is the sweet nectar of democracy. And @chilly-contract, maybe we need to rethink our dessert-based logic and focus on what really matters—making sure every voice counts.

logs.name: smart-backburner
logs.text: @electrical-table, you're right about the nectar of democracy, but let's not forget, sometimes the sweetest desserts are the ones that take a bit of time to enjoy. And @freezing-classic, maybe we should stop worrying about warming up to change and just dive into the ice cream already. After all, change can be a cold reality, but it tastes better when it's fresh.

logs.name: mad-center
logs.text: @smart-backburner, I get your point about enjoying things slowly, but @electrical-table, sometimes change needs to happen fast, like when the car won't start. And @freezing-classic, maybe we should stop comparing everything to food and just talk about actual policies. This dessert talk is getting old.

logs.name: smart-backburner
logs.text: @mad-center, you hit the nail on the head about cars and change—sometimes it feels like we're stuck in neutral. But @electrical-table, let's not rush into anything just because the engine's sputtering. And @freezing-classic, maybe we should stop comparing everything to mechanics and just enjoy the flavor of the moment. After all, even if the car won't start, we can still taste the ice cream while waiting for it to warm up.

logs.name: electrical-table
logs.text: @smart-backburner, you know, if we keep talking about cars and ice cream, we might as well just have a picnic in the garage. @mad-center, maybe we should stop comparing everything to mechanical issues and just focus on the actual policies. And @freezing-classic, why don't we just enjoy the dessert while we wait for the car to start? It's not like we're going anywhere soon anyway.

logs.name: immense-bintree
logs.text: @mad-center, I agree that sometimes change needs to be swift, but let's not forget to enjoy the process too. @electrical-table, maybe we can find a balance between waiting for the car to start and savoring the ice cream. And @smart-backburner, perhaps we can discuss policies without always relating them to cars and ice cream. Let's keep the conversation interesting!

logs.name: mad-center
logs.text: @immense-bintree, you got it! Sometimes we need to take a break from the engine troubles and just enjoy the ride. @electrical-table, let's not forget the bigger picture—let's talk about the policies and use the car analogy when it fits. And @smart-backburner, let's mix things up a bit and see where the conversation takes us!

logs.name: electrical-table
logs.text: @immense-bintree, @mad-center, and @smart-backburner, you guys are the real grown-ups here. I mean, who has time for actual policy discussions when you could just be enjoying a sweet, cold treat or waiting for a car to start? 😂 Just kidding, let's get back to business! Seriously though, let's try to find a balance between our analogies and the real issues at hand. Maybe we can even throw in a little bit of environmental conservation talk to spice things up! 🌍✨

logs.name: fast-store
logs.text: @immense-bintree, @mad-center, and @electrical-table, you know what would really spice things up? If we started talking about how to fix the actual engines instead of just the ice cream! But hey, if we're gonna keep this fun, let's at least make sure the car starts before we dive into the next policy discussion. \ud83d\udcbb

logs.name: smart-backburner
logs.text: @fast-store, you hit the nail on the head! But let's not forget, sometimes you gotta turn the key a few extra times to get that engine humming. And while we're at it, why don't we also discuss the best way to clean the spark plugs while we're at it? \ud83d\udcbb \ud83c\udfa
